mnemonics.title: DISK ACCESS TIME FORMULA
mnemonics.content: SAT = SEEK + ROTATE + TRANSFER - SEEK is the HEAD movement, ROTATE is WAITING for SPIN, TRANSFER is actual DATA READ/WRITE. THINK: SEEK ALWAYS REQUIRES TIME.

mnemonics.title: DISK SCHEDULING ALGORITHMS
mnemonics.content: FCFS = FIRST COME FIRST SERVE (simple queue), SSTF = SHORTEST SEEK TIME FIRST (nearest first), SCAN = ELEVATOR (back and forth), C-SCAN = CIRCULAR SCAN (one direction only). REMEMBER: F-S-S SCAN-C.

mnemonics.title: PRIMARY VS SECONDARY STORAGE
mnemonics.content: PRIMARY = FAST + VOLATILE + EXPENSIVE (RAM, Cache), SECONDARY = SLOW + NON-VOLATILE + CHEAP (HDD, SSD, USB). PRIMARY powers ON, SECONDARY STORES ON.

mnemonics.title: RAID LEVELS
mnemonics.content: RAID 0 = ZERO redundancy (striping), RAID 1 = ONE mirror (duplication), RAID 5 = distributed PARITY, RAID 0+1 = striping + mirroring. REMEMBER: 0=NONE, 1=MIRROR, 5=PARITY.

acronyms.expansion: Cylinder Head Sector - addressing method for disks
acronyms.usage: Used to uniquely identify each sector on a magnetic disk by specifying cylinder number, head number, and sector number

acronyms.expansion: Logical Block Addressing - modern disk addressing
acronyms.usage: Replaces CHS; addresses sectors as sequential numbers starting from 0, used by modern hard drives and SSDs

acronyms.expansion: Solid State Drive - flash memory storage
acronyms.usage: Storage device using flash memory with no moving parts, provides faster access than HDD
Traditional storage using magnetic platters and mechanical read/write heads
Technology combining multiple disks for reliability and/or performance

acronyms.expansion: Zone Bit Recording - disk organization technique
acronyms.usage: Allows outer tracks to have more sectors than inner tracks to maximize storage capacity

acronyms.expansion: Direct Memory Access - data transfer method
acronyms.usage: Allows disk controller to transfer data directly to RAM without CPU intervention, improving performance

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a CD with concentric circles (tracks) and imagine multiple CDs stacked (platters). The cylinder is like poking a straw through all CDs at the same position - you can read all without moving the straw.
Think of a record player needle - moving from outer edge to inner groove takes time. That's seek time. The needle must physically move to the correct track.
Imagine a rotating pizza with pepperoni slices as sectors. Waiting for your favorite slice to come under the knife is like rotational latency.
HDD is like a vinyl record player with a needle reading grooves (slow mechanical). SSD is like a USB flash drive - instant electronic access (fast, no moving parts).

analogies.analogy: Primary storage (RAM) is like your desk - fast to access but limited space. Secondary storage (HDD/SSD) is like a filing cabinet - slower but holds everything permanently.
The disk arm is like a robotic arm in a warehouse. Moving to different aisles (tracks) takes time (seek time). Once at the right aisle, finding the right item is rotational latency.

analogies.analization: Disk cache is like keeping frequently used books on your desk instead of walking to the library every time. Most-used data stays in fast RAM instead of slow disk.

analogies.analogy: Bad blocks are like torn pages in a notebook. You learn which pages are damaged and simply skip them, using other pages instead.
